title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Dubu Jorim: Easy & Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Master Dubu Jorim, the classic Korean spicy braised tofu. This easy vegetarian recipe delivers incredible flavor with simple ingredients. Perfect for a quick weeknight meal!
longTailKeywords: how to make Dubu Jorim spicy braised tofu
seo.h1: Authentic Dubu Jorim: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
h2Headings: What is Dubu Jorim (Korean Spicy Braised Tofu)?
Ingredients You'll Need for Dubu Jorim
Step-by-Step Guide to Making Dubu Jorim
Pro Tips for Perfect Spicy Braised Tofu
seo.imageAlt: A vibrant bowl of Dubu Jorim, spicy Korean braised tofu, garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Dive into the rich, spicy flavors of homemade Dubu Jorim, a beloved Korean vegetarian dish that's surprisingly easy to make!

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean classic featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a rich, spicy, and savory braising sauce. This comforting vegetarian dish offers a delightful balance of textures and flavors, making it a perfect hearty side or main course. It's incredibly satisfying and surprisingly easy to prepare at home.

instructions.title: Prepare the Tofu
instructions.text: Press the tofu block for at least 30 minutes to remove excess water. This helps it absorb flavor and achieve a firmer texture. Slice the pressed tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles.

instructions.title: Make the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium bowl, combine soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, 1 chopped green onion stalk, sesame oil, and sugar or corn syrup. Whisk until well combined. Set aside.

instructions.title: Pan-Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Heat vegetable oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer and pan-fry for 3-4 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. Do not overcrowd the pan; cook in batches if necessary.

instructions.title: Combine and Simmer
instructions.text: Once all tofu is pan-fried, arrange the browned tofu slices back into the pan in an even layer. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Add the water or vegetable broth to the pan.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Reduce heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 15-20 minutes, allowing the tofu to absorb the rich flavors. Periodically spoon the sauce over the tofu.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the lid and continue to simmer for another 5 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened slightly to your liking. Garnish generously with the remaining chopped green onions and toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with a bowl of warm rice.

tips: Always press your tofu thoroughly. This step is crucial for achieving the best texture and allowing the tofu to soak up the delicious braising sauce.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru to control the spice level. For a milder dish, reduce it to 1 tablespoon; for extra heat, add another half tablespoon.
Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors meld further. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days.
article:
## Discover the Irresistible Flavor of Dubu Jorim: Your Guide to Korean Spicy Braised Tofu

In the vibrant tapestry of Korean cuisine, few dishes capture the essence of comfort, flavor, and health quite like Dubu Jorim. This beloved vegetarian staple, known as spicy braised tofu, is a testament to how simple ingredients can transform into an extraordinary culinary experience. Whether you’re a seasoned fan of Korean food or just beginning your journey into its rich flavors, Dubu Jorim offers a deliciously accessible entry point. It’s hearty, incredibly satisfying, and packed with a fiery kick that’s perfectly balanced by the subtle creaminess of tofu. Prepare to dive into the world of this iconic dish and learn how to bring its authentic taste right to your kitchen.

## What is Dubu Jorim (Korean Spicy Braised Tofu)?

Dubu Jorim directly translates to "braised tofu," with the "jorim" referring to a Korean cooking technique involving simmering ingredients in a flavorful, often savory and spicy, sauce until it thickens and coats the main components. In this case, firm or extra-firm tofu is pan-fried until golden brown, then gently braised in a mouth-watering concoction typically made from gochujang (Korean chili paste), gochugaru (Korean chili flakes), soy sauce, garlic, green onions, and a touch of sweetness.

Historically, tofu has been a vital source of protein in East Asian diets, and dishes like Dubu Jorim showcase its versatility and ability to absorb bold flavors. It’s a quintessential banchan (side dish) often found on Korean tables, but it's substantial enough to be enjoyed as a main course, especially for those seeking a delicious vegetarian or vegan option. Its appeal lies in its contrast: the crispy exterior of the pan-fried tofu yields to a tender interior, all enveloped in a deeply savory, umami-rich, and wonderfully spicy sauce.

## Ingredients You'll Need for Dubu Jorim

Crafting authentic Dubu Jorim requires a few key ingredients, many of which are staples in Korean pantries. Here’s what you’ll need to gather:

**For the Tofu:**
*   1 block (14-16 oz) firm or extra-firm tofu
*   2-3 tablespoons cooking oil (e.g., vegetable, canola)

**For the Braising Sauce:**
*   2 tablespoons soy sauce
*   1 tablespoon gochujang (Korean chili paste)
*   1 tablespoon gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) - adjust to your spice preference
*   1 tablespoon mirin (rice wine) or sugar for sweetness
*   1 tablespoon minced garlic
*   1 teaspoon sesame oil
*   1/2 cup water or vegetable broth
*   1/4 cup chopped green onions (for sauce and garnish)
*   1 teaspoon toasted sesame seeds (for garnish, optional)

**Optional Additions for more flavor/texture:**
*   Sliced onion or mushrooms for added depth
*   A pinch of black pepper

Ensure your tofu is well-pressed to remove excess water; this step is crucial for achieving that desirable crispy exterior and allowing the tofu to better absorb the sauce.

## Step-by-Step Guide to Making Dubu Jorim

Making Dubu Jorim is surprisingly straightforward. Follow these steps for a perfect batch:

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:** Drain the tofu and wrap it in several layers of paper towels or a clean kitchen towel. Place a heavy object (like a cast-iron pan or cutting board) on top for at least 30 minutes to press out excess water. Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles.
2.  **Pan-Fry the Tofu:** Heat 2-3 tablespoons of cooking oil in a large non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side, or until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove the tofu from the pan and set aside.
3.  **Prepare the Sauce:** In a bowl, whisk together the soy sauce, gochujang, gochugaru, mirin (or sugar), minced garlic, sesame oil, and water (or vegetable broth). Add about half of the chopped green onions to the sauce mixture.
4.  **Braise the Tofu:** In the same skillet (no need to clean it), reduce the heat to medium. Pour in the prepared sauce. Once the sauce begins to simmer, gently add the pan-fried tofu slices back into the skillet, arranging them in a single layer.
5.  **Simmer and Thicken:** Spoon the sauce over the tofu slices to coat them evenly. Let the tofu braise in the sauce for 8-10 minutes, flipping once halfway through, until the sauce has thickened and reduced, thoroughly coating the tofu.
6.  **Garnish and Serve:** Transfer the Dubu Jorim to a serving plate. Garnish with the remaining fresh green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately.

## Pro Tips for Perfect Spicy Braised Tofu

To elevate your Dubu Jorim from good to absolutely unforgettable, consider these expert tips:

*   **Press Your Tofu Thoroughly:** This is arguably the most critical step. Well-pressed tofu won't be watery, will crisp up better when pan-fried, and will absorb the braising sauce more effectively, leading to a richer flavor and texture.
*   **Don't Skimp on the Gochujang and Gochugaru:** These two ingredients are the heart of the sauce. Adjust the amounts to your preferred spice level, but remember they provide not just heat but also a complex depth of flavor and beautiful color.
*   **Achieve a Good Sear:** Frying the tofu until it's golden brown and slightly crispy adds a fantastic textural contrast to the soft interior once it's braised.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Before adding the tofu, taste your braising sauce and adjust seasonings. Need more sweetness? Add a touch more sugar or mirin. Want it spicier? A little more gochugaru. Too salty? A splash more water.
*   **Gentle Simmering:** Allow the sauce to slowly reduce and thicken, coating each piece of tofu. Don't rush this step, as it allows the flavors to meld beautifully.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile and pairs wonderfully with a variety of Korean dishes. Its robust flavor makes it a fantastic accompaniment to a simple bowl of freshly steamed white or brown rice, which helps to soak up every last drop of the delicious sauce.

As a banchan, it complements other Korean staples like:
*   **Kimchi:** The fermented spice of kimchi creates a harmonious balance with the fresh spice of the Dubu Jorim.
*   **Other Vegetable Banchan:** Think seasoned spinach (sigeumchi namul), bean sprout salad (kongnamul muchim), or cucumber salad (oi muchim) for a refreshing contrast.
*   **Soups and Stews:** Pair it with a mild doenjang jjigae (soybean paste stew) or a refreshing seaweed soup (miyeokguk) to round out a full Korean meal.

For a complete vegetarian meal, simply serve a generous portion of Dubu Jorim over rice with a few light vegetable side dishes.

## Dubu Jorim Variations & Substitutions

While the classic Dubu Jorim is perfect as is, you can certainly experiment with variations to suit your taste or dietary needs:

*   **Less Spicy:** Reduce the amount of gochugaru or omit it entirely if you prefer a milder flavor. You can also add a bit more sugar or mirin to balance the heat.
*   **Add Vegetables:** Enhance the dish by adding quick-cooking vegetables during the braising process. Sliced onions, mushrooms (shiitake or cremini), zucchini, or even bell peppers can add extra flavor, nutrition, and texture. Add them a few minutes before the tofu so they cook through.
*   **Different Tofu Textures:** While firm or extra-firm is recommended for braising, some enjoy medium-firm tofu for an even softer texture. Just be more gentle when handling it.
*   **Vegan Friendly:** Dubu Jorim is inherently vegan, assuming you use vegetable broth instead of any animal-based broth (which is rare in this dish anyway). Always double-check your gochujang and other sauces for animal products, though most standard brands are vegan.
*   **Gluten-Free Option:** Use tamari instead of regular soy sauce to make this dish entirely gluten-free.

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

**Q: Is Dubu Jorim vegan?**
A: Yes, traditional Dubu Jorim is typically vegan as it uses tofu, vegetables, and plant-based sauces. Always check the ingredients of your gochujang and soy sauce to be certain, but most standard brands are vegan.

**Q: How can I make Dubu Jorim less spicy?**
A: To reduce the spiciness, simply decrease the amount of gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) or omit it entirely. You can also increase the amount of mirin or sugar to balance the heat with sweetness.

**Q: Can I prepare the tofu ahead of time?**
A: You can press and pan-fry the tofu a day in advance and store it in an airtight container in the refrigerator. When ready to serve, simply prepare the sauce and braise the pre-cooked tofu.

**Q: How do I store leftovers?**
A: Leftover Dubu Jorim can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days. Reheat gently on the stovetop or in the microwave. The flavors often deepen overnight!

**Q: What kind of tofu is best for Dubu Jorim?**
A: Firm or extra-firm tofu is highly recommended. Its denser texture holds up well to pressing, pan-frying, and braising, preventing it from crumbling in the sauce.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's a culinary journey into the heart of Korean flavors. With its perfect blend of spicy, savory, and subtly sweet notes, this braised tofu dish proves that vegetarian food can be incredibly satisfying and bursting with character. So gather your ingredients, follow these steps, and prepare to delight your taste buds with this authentic and utterly delicious Korean classic.
